items.title: Petrol stations tried to 'shape' the market – now it's shaping them

children.content: Petrol stations in our biggest cities hiked prices ahead of global oil prices, but experts say their profit margins are now much slimmer.

articles.title: Fuel purchasing powers could be used for nearly anything facing supply disruption

children.content: There are concerns future governments could abuse broad powers that are designed to support companies to buy more fuel but could be used on any good facing supply-chain disruption.

image.altText: Fuel tanker

articles.title: How is the war in Iran impacting you or your business? Share with us

children.content: As fuel costs soar and the global economy reacts to a shortage of oil supplies, we need your help in identifying the impacts here in Australia. Whether it's the cost of your work commute or supply shortages affecting your business, share it with us.

image.altText: A row of petrol pumps.

items.title: Early government modelling shows how long fuel stockpile lasts if imports slow

image.altText: A man in a suit looks across the camera with an australian flag in the background.

children.content: Diesel stocks would last about six months if Australia's imports dropped by 20 per cent, according to modelling compiled for the federal government early in the Middle East war.

items.title: Surcharges on debit and credit cards to go from October

children.content: The removal of surcharges on debit and credit cards is one of the sweeping reforms from the Reserve Bank.

image.altText: A person taps a black credit or debit card on a payment terminal.

items.title: A minute before Trump's wild Iran claim, oil traders made 'abnormal' moves

children.content: Global stock markets, which for the past month have been remarkably restrained as the Middle East crisis unfolded, have begun to wobble.

image.altText: A satellite view of the Strait of Hormuz.

items.title: Israel backs down over holy site, Pope rebukes Hegseth, and what else you should know

children.content: Donald Trump renews threats against Iran's energy infrastructure while his secretary of war faces an apparent rebuke from the pope.

articles.title: Trump threatens to 'obliterate' Kharg Island if Iran refuses deal to end war

children.content: As thousands more US troops arrive in the Middle East, Donald Trump threatens to wipe out Iran's energy plants and oil wells if it does not reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

image.altText: Donald Trump standing in a doorway aboard Air Force One.

articles.title: Israel passes death penalty for Palestinians convicted of lethal attacks

children.content: The law, long-sought by Israel's far-right politicians, makes death by hanging the default punishment for West Bank Palestinians convicted of killing Israelis.

image.altText: Benjamin Netanyahu looking stern at a press conference.

articles.title: Albanese wants Trump to recognise damage Iran war has caused

children.content: Anthony Albanese wants "more certainty" from the United States on its war objectives in Iran and says the administration must be clear if it is still pursuing regime change.

image.altText: Albanese on Trump and Iran war TN

items.title: Spain closes airspace to US planes involved in Middle East war

image.altText: The front of  a grey plane with the word U.S Air Force painted in black paint.

children.content: White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt says US President Donald Trump is interested in getting Arab countries to pay for the war in the Middle East. Follow live.

items.title: Wong reiterates Australia will not provide boots on ground in Iran war

children.content: Foreign Minister Penny Wong says Australia has been clear that it will not provide troops on the ground for an operation against Iran. Follow live.

image.altText: a lady standing holding her arm out

items.title: Iran's 'safe passage' signals a new global divide

children.content: China is now the world's largest crude oil importer, and Iran — though a discounted and often opaque supplier — remains a consistent part of that supply mix.

image.altText: China President Xi Jinping.

items.title: From Porepunkah to Thologolong: The long search for Dezi Freeman

children.content: When the parents of Senior Constable Vadim de Waart-Hottart flew to Melbourne for a police service, little did they know their son's killer would emerge just days later.

articles.title: Police continue hunt for anyone who assisted fugitive Dezi Freeman

children.content: Police say they will come down hard on anyone who helped Dezi Freeman, who was shot dead by police yesterday after a massive seven-month manhunt.

items.title: How one man's stolen identity was used to run a cult

children.content: In mid-2009 British citizen Simon Kadwill-Kelly was catching a ferry returning from France. Within minutes of trying to board he found out his identity had been stolen.

items.title: Transphobia nearly killed Kaz. Now he just wants to be 'me'

children.content: This WA teenager was depressed, ill and missing out on school. He says it was part of the toll transphobia took on him.

image.altText: A boy stands in front of ocean scenery.

items.title: Vaping likely to cause cancer, new Australian review of evidence finds

children.content: Nicotine-based vapes are likely to cause lung and oral cancer, according to a new review of evidence. Experts say there needs to be tougher enforcement of vape laws in Australia.

articles.title: It's $2.5m a dose and it's giving some children another chance at life

children.content: Children born with a rare neuromuscular disorder are being given another chance in life thanks to a new treatment experts are calling a "game-changer".

articles.title: 'I'm sick of it': One in two Australians priced out of healthcare system

children.content: One in two Australians missed out on health care they needed last year mainly because they could not afford it, according to a new report by the peak healthcare consumers group.

items.title: Céline Dion to perform again after years-long health-related hiatus

children.content: The My Heart Will Go On singer disclosed in 2022 that she had a rare neurological disease, indefinitely cancelling her shows.

items.title: How to tell if you have insufficient insurance for your home and contents

children.content: If your home is underinsured, it means your insurance policy won't fully cover the cost of rebuilding, repairing or replacing it.

items.title: This luminous film shows a side of Iraq many Westerners have never seen

children.content: Filmmaker Hasan Hadi had to bring flowers to school to celebrate Saddam Hussein’s birthday. That memory loosely informs his beautiful debut feature, The President’s Cake.

items.title: Five social media companies investigated over response to teen ban

items.title: Businesses report increase in abuse from customers as fuel crisis worsens

items.title: 'You are just an immigrant': Millions in unpaid wages, modern slavery uncovered

children.content: Millions in stolen wages, threats of visa cancellation and modern slavery by Australian employers have been uncovered in the first year of a national program.

items.title: Draft report into ANU's controversial cuts critical of university's council

children.content: ANU's council approved a massive $250 million cost-cutting program without clear evidence it was needed or achievable, according to an assessment in a draft audit office report.

items.title: Black robed lawyers and 'killer' beef at Kimberley court

items.title: Concerns about light and noise from $1b outback energy project resolved

children.content: The battery project, which will provide back-up power for up to 80,000 homes in Broken Hill, will go ahead with new conditions after a dispute was resolved between the company and an outback stargazing business.

items.title: Wine manufacturer De Bortoli has its own solution for workers amidst housing crisis
